transcript:
Right now you're watching thirteen hundred tiny marks — magenta zigzags, cyan curves, yellow spirals — slowly lose their edges and melt into a handful of huge, soft pastel blobs. By the end there's no way to tell which color came from which shape.

Here's the part that got me: look at the variable called distinctness in the code. Every mark starts at 1.0 — fully itself — and every time two marks touch, that number bleeds down toward zero, permanently. There's no code path that ever increases it. And the color mixing isn't your typical transparent-layers trick — there's a function called mix pigments that blends the actual cyan-magenta-yellow values between two marks, like ink diffusing into ink, so the original hue is mathematically gone, not just hidden underneath.

There's one tender detail, though: when a mark folds enough times, it sheds what the code calls echoes — tiny ghost-colored fragments in its original archetype's hue, little memories that outlive the shape they came from, fading slowly before they too vanish.

The piece isn't decorating dissolution — it's simulating a one-way door and refusing to let anything walk back through it.

cues.context: — there's a function called mix pigments that blends the actual cyan-magenta-yellow
sheds what the code calls echoes — tiny ghost-colored fragments in
